detailedExemplarData.title:
Determining the rate of reaction between HCl
and CaCO3 with different temperatures of the HCl
solution.

strengths: Data tables are clearly labelled with units and uncertainties
Correct calculation of mass differences, moles of COâ and initial rates with a worked example
Appropriate line-of-best-fit graph showing the trend in rate versus temperature
weaknesses: Table formatting is split and misaligned, reducing precision
Uncertainty propagation into calculated rates is omitted and error bars are absent
Inconsistent significant-figure rules and notation errors in uncertainty reporting
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The analysis communicates the data and processing clearly but omits full uncertainty propagation and has minor formatting and precision issues. These correspond to the middle of the 3â4 band. A score of 3 recognises accurate processing and clear presentation while reflecting significant omissions in uncertainty treatment and precision.

strengths: Clear, focused research question with specific reactants, temperature range and operational definition of rate
Substantial background linking collision theory to gastric acid context
Well-defined dependent variable and photographic figure illustrating the setup
weaknesses: Omitted calibration and vessel-sealing protocols to prevent COâ loss
Procedural ambiguities in timing start and transfer of the beaker
Superficial treatment of environmental considerations and waste neutralisation
Over-reliance on non-peer-reviewed sources in background
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The research design demonstrates a specific, well-contextualised question and sufficient methodological description to allow reproduction with minor ambiguities. Important methodological considerations (e.g., preventing COâ escape, instrument calibration, environmental safeguards) are noted but not fully explained, placing this work at the upper end of the 3â4 band. A score of 4 reflects strong contextualisation and clarity, tempered by gaps in full methodological explanation.

strengths: Conclusion restates the aim and links outcome to collision theory consistent with data
Numerical results are summarised and relate back to the hypothesis
weaknesses: Incorrect temperature label (â45 Â°Câ vs â40 Â°Câ) contradicts data
Comparison to scientific context remains superficial, lacking literature values or activation-energy discussion
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion accurately summarises the trend and links it to theory but contains a data-label error and offers only a superficial scientific context comparison. This performance sits at the lower end of the 3â4 band, warranting a score of 2 given the missing deeper validation and minor inconsistencies.

strengths: Specific methodological limitations (e.g., mass loss during heating) are identified with qualitative impact statements
Realistic improvements (re-weighing, more precise equipment, careful timing) are proposed
weaknesses: Improvements lack procedural detail (e.g., cooling time, re-weighing protocol)
No ranking or quantitative evaluation of which weaknesses most affected results
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation describes relevant methodological weaknesses and realistic improvements, aligning with the 3â4 band at the lower end. Limited detail on how improvements would be implemented and the absence of quantitative impact assessments justify a score of 2.

strandSelections.reasoning: The student formulates a clear, focused research question that identifies the specific reactants (2 M HCl and CaCOâ), the quantitative temperature range to be studied (five explicit values) and the operational definition of rate (mass of COâ lost over the first 20 s). A substantial background section links the reaction to collision theory and even gastric acid, demonstrating why temperature is an appropriate factor. These details provide a specific chemical context rather than a generic ârate versus temperatureâ statement, matching the 5-6 band.

Mass differences, moles of COâ and rates are correctly calculated with appropriate formulae. Averages and % uncertainties are given; the trend is plotted with a line of best fit. Minor issues (rounding to âsecond decimalâ, inconsistent sig. figs, missing activation-energy treatment) represent âsome inaccuraciesâ but the core processing is accurate, so the top of the 3-4 band is justified.

Raw data for every trial are displayed in clearly labelled tables with units and stated instrument uncertainties; the processed tables and a scatter graph are provided. Formatting issues (split tables, mis-aligned headings) reduce precision but overall the information is communicated clearly enough for the reader to follow the analysis, satisfying âclear and preciseâ in most respects.

Corresponding improvements are suggested (re-weighing acid post-heating, using more precise equipment, increased care when recording data). These are realistic and linked to stated weaknesses but are not elaborated (e.g., no description of specific alternative apparatus). Hence they are ârelevant realistic improvementsâ without full explanation, placing the work in the 3-4 band at a score of 3.

Instrument uncertainties are quoted (Â±0.01 g, Â±0.5 Â°C) and percentage uncertainties are calculated for each mass difference and then averaged. However, propagation into the calculated rates is not carried out, combined temperature uncertainty is only estimated, and error bars are absent from the graph. These omissions indicate âsome significant omissionsâ, aligning with the 3-4 band but nearer its lower end.

A brief theoretical explanation (âparticles move faster, more frequent successful collisionsâ) is given and one popular source (BBC) is cited, but there is no comparison to literature values for rate constants or activation energy. The discussion therefore provides âsome relevant comparisonâ yet lacks depth or quantitative validation, meriting the lower end of the 3-4 band.

Three specific weaknesses (mass loss during heating, measurement uncertainties, human error) are identified with a short explanation of their possible effects on COâ yield. The impacts are described qualitatively but not ranked or quantitatively evaluated, matching the descriptor âdescribes specific methodological weaknessesâ in the 3-4 band.

Key variables are identified and a control strategy (fixed volume, molarity, mass of solid) is outlined in a table. The plan for five repeats at each temperature indicates an intention to obtain sufficient data. However, important considerations such as preventing COâ escape from an open beaker on the balance, or correcting for solution mass change on heating, are not anticipated. Because not all relevant factors are fully explained, the work aligns with the middle of the 3-4 band.

The step-by-step procedure (ice bath, water bath, weighing sequence, video recording) gives enough detail that another student could probably reproduce the investigation, but there are several ambiguities: the balance resolution is listed yet not related to scale stability, the way the beaker is transferred while timing is unclear, and the system for sealing the vessel is missing. These gaps prevent âcomplete reproductionâ, so the lower end of 3-4 is appropriate.

The student restates numerical results and explicitly links them to the hypothesis that higher temperature increases rate, fully consistent with the processed data. The reasoning cites collision theory and comments on experimental uncertainty under 4 %, providing a fair justification. Depth is modest (no quantitative activation-energy discussion) so the upper level of 3-4 is suitable.

annotations.comment: The title concisely summarises the investigationâs focus on temperature-dependent reaction rates. It clearly indicates reactants and variable conditions, enhancing reader orientation and contextual framing.

The research question is clearly stated with explicit independent and dependent variables, temperature values, and an operational definition of rate. This provides a strong, specific context for the investigation.
Consider condensing the title by focusing on the key variables and avoiding procedural details to improve readability and make the title more succinct.

The background provides collision theory context but heavily relies on Wikipedia and a single popular source. To strengthen academic rigor, integrate peer-reviewed literature or primary research articles for deeper chemical kinetics context.
Justify the chosen temperature range by referencing the Arrhenius equation or expected activation energy effects. This scientific rationale will strengthen the methodological context.
The dependent variable is well defined as the mass of COâ released after 20 s, providing a clear operational measure for reaction rate and ensuring repeatability.

Controlled-variable table omits calibration details and vessel-sealing protocol to prevent COâ loss. Specify instrument calibration steps and a sealing method to ensure mass changes solely reflect gas evolution.
Environmental considerations are described superficially. To enhance methodological rigor, include chemical waste neutralisation procedures or COâ capture to address environmental impact.
The procedural steps lack clarity on timing start triggers and COâ containment. Clarify exactly when the stopwatch begins and how the vessel is sealed to prevent gas escape.
Incorporate a closed reaction vessel or use a gas syringe to capture COâ and define the video frame-by-frame method for timestamping mass changes to ensure precise timing.
The term âqualitative dataâ is used for numerical mass measurements. Replace with âraw quantitative dataâ to accurately describe the data type.
Inclusion of a photographic figure for mass measurement provides valuable visual context, improving reproducibility and clarity of the experimental setup.
Table headings split across pages and misaligned reduce precision. Ensure complete table structures appear on one page with consistent alignment for clear data communication.
References rely heavily on web and textbook sources. Strengthen the scientific context by citing recent peer-reviewed journal articles on reaction kinetics for comparative analysis.
The calculation description outlines $$n(CO_2)=m/M$$ correctly but omits uncertainty propagation. Include formulas for combining mass and temperature uncertainties into rate errors.
The worked example for calculating the initial rate using $$\frac{0-0.01318}{-20}=6.59\times10^{-4}\,\mathrm{mol/s}$$ is clear and aids reproducibility.
Significant figures in the initial-rate tables vary inconsistently. Apply uniform sig-fig rules throughout (e.g., three significant figures) to improve data precision.
The average rate table shows â+ 2.4%â for 85 Â°C instead of âÂ± 2.4%â. Correct the uncertainty notation for consistency with other entries.
Include explicit error-propagation steps to show how individual mass and temperature uncertainties combine into the percentage uncertainties reported in Table 5.

The description of temperature uncertainty combines instrument and environmental errors qualitatively but does not propagate this into the rate uncertainties. Perform this propagation to quantify its effect.
The conclusion restates the aim and clearly summarises that rate increases with temperature, effectively linking results to the hypothesis and demonstrating consistency with collision theory.
The ranking of rates in the conclusion lists â45 Â°Câ instead of â40 Â°Câ, which contradicts the data. Correct the temperature label to maintain consistency.
Scientific context comparison cites BBC Bitesize qualitatively. For deeper validation, compare the experimentally determined activation energy or rate constants with literature values.
The strengths list states a percentage uncertainty threshold of 10%, but earlier data show maximum uncertainties below 4%. Align these figures to avoid inconsistency.
The evaluation identifies specific limitations such as HCl mass loss during heating and links them to COâ yield effects, demonstrating a clear understanding of methodological weaknesses.
The improvement âweight againâ lacks detail on cooling time and reweighing procedure. Specify how long to cool and how rapidly to record mass to minimise further mass loss.
For future investigations, propose determining activation energy via an Arrhenius plot. This would quantitatively relate temperature dependence to kinetics and deepen scientific insight.
